ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for prawn mango masala curry; 'curry dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as the closest broader class. Chapati component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the primary protein anchor for the prawn masala component; chapati component maps to FoodEx2 A00LC (unleavened flatbread) but no composite dish code is available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for prawn mango masala with chapati. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component. Chapati aligns with FoodEx2 A00LC (unleavened flatbread) but a single composite dish code is unavailable in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional South Indian curry preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of prawn mango masala curry (roughly 250 g) and two soft whole-wheat chapatis (roughly 200 g combined), as typically served in Singapore Indian restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — composite estimation for prawn mango masala with chapati
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for prawn curry and whole-wheat chapati components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and whole-wheat chapati/roti (FDC ID 175032). The input values (energy 144.44 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.22 g, carbohydrate 14.44 g per 100 g) are consistent with a weighted blend of approximately 55% prawn curry and 45% chapati by mass and are retained as the best available estimate. Micronutrients are cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp and whole-wheat flatbread entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — whole-wheat chapati and mixed seafood curry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for eraal maangai masala with chapati. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on: (1) whole-wheat chapati GI ~52 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021, chapati made from whole-wheat atta); (2) prawn masala curry component has negligible independent GI contribution due to very low carbohydrate content. The composite dish GI is dominated by the chapati component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate from chapati portion (~200 g × ~25 g/100 g = ~50 g available carb) / 100 ≈ 16.8. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to one serving and request reduced added salt during preparation, as the sodium content per full serving is clinically significant; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Whole-wheat chapati provides a low GI (~52) and dietary fibre (2.22 g/100 g), which moderates postprandial glucose rise; however, a 450 g serving delivers approximately 65 g total carbohydrate, requiring careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in a controlled portion of one chapati with a half-serving of curry to limit total carbohydrate to approximately 30–35 g per meal, leveraging the low GI of whole-wheat chapati and the protein content of prawns to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 55.56 mg/100 g (approximately 250 mg per 450 g serving) from prawns, combined with sodium at 266.67 mg/100 g; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.78 g/100 g, which is a favourable factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size due to the elevated cholesterol contribution from prawns (~250 mg per full serving, approaching the 300 mg daily threshold), while noting that the low saturated fat content and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) are cardioprotective; sodium reduction during cooking is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (tomato, green chilli) may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their personal tolerance to nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) present in the masala base, and may consider reducing or omitting these ingredients while retaining turmeric and ginger for their anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eraal Maangai Masala with Chapati is a South Indian Tamil dish popular in Singapore's Indian hawker stalls and restaurants, consisting of succulent prawns (eraal) simmered in a tangy, spiced gravy made with raw mango (maangai), onion, tomato, turmeric, chilli, ginger, and garlic, served alongside two soft, unleavened whole-wheat chapatis. The curry has a bold, sour-spicy flavour profile from the raw mango, balanced by the earthiness of the spice blend and the natural sweetness of the prawns. The chapatis are pan-cooked on a tawa until lightly charred and pliable, providing a neutral, slightly nutty counterpoint to the tangy curry. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal per 100 g, with 7.78 g protein from the prawns, 14.44 g carbohydrate primarily from the chapati, and 6.22 g fat. Dietary fibre is 2.22 g/100 g from the whole-wheat chapati. Sodium is elevated at 266.67 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. Cholesterol from prawns is approximately 55.56 mg/100 g. The dish has a low glycaemic index (~52) due to the whole-wheat chapati base, making it a moderate-GI meal overall. Clinically, it is suitable for most adults in controlled portions, with caution advised for individuals with hypertension (sodium), cardiovascular disease (cholesterol), and rheumatoid arthritis (nightshade vegetables). The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger add functional food value. This dish is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian establishments and is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for prawn curry and whole-wheat chapati; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. The input values are internally consistent with a plausible component blend and are retained as the best available estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on whole-wheat chapati GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite prawn mango masala with chapati dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry; component-level fatty acid data would require weighted blending not performed here. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific composite dish in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single record; individual component data exists but composite blending was not performed. 3. No zero-macro justification required: all core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 6.22, carbohydrate_g 14.44) are non-zero. 4. Ethnic tag 'Biryani' from input removed as it is not applicable to this dish. 5. The dish is classified as South Indian Tamil cuisine, originating from the Tamil community in Singapore; country_of_origin is Singapore. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the use of prawns and plant-based ingredients with no pork or alcohol; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in small amounts from cooking oils and is retained. 8. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy G5 hard gate requirement that foodex2_match_type must be one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
